title: Kirigami simple pop-up
subtitle: Cutting and folding a single-sheet kirigami pop-up that stands when the card opens
excerpt: A kirigami pop-up is made from a single sheet of paper by combining cuts and folds. When the card is opened, the cut sections rise away from the plane of the card. The basic pop-up unit is a parallel-fold tab: two parallel cuts from the fold, with a connecting fold across them. All more complex kirigami structures are extensions of this unit.
sourceNotes: Kirigami pop-up mechanics are documented in contemporary paper engineering literature. The parallel-fold tab is the foundational unit described in Paul Jackson, Cut and Fold Techniques for Pop-Up Designs (2014). No pre-1928 source covers kirigami as distinct from origami. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The basic kirigami pop-up unit: two parallel cuts from the spine fold, with a mountain fold across them parallel to the spine, which causes the tab to spring forward when the card opens.
The central fold of a card or sheet from which a pop-up mechanism is cut. The mechanism always attaches to the spine fold.

content.text: Every kirigami pop-up derives from the same mechanical principle: a cut section that is mountain-folded across its width rises away from the card plane when the card is opened. The
is the starting point. Understanding how the tab rises allows more complex cuts to be designed from first principles.
Cut on a self-healing mat. Keep fingers clear of the ruler edge when using a craft knife. Score fold lines with the bone folder before folding to prevent tearing.
Card stock, 160 to 240 gsm. Thin card (below 150 gsm) produces a limp pop-up that does not hold its shape when the card opens. Heavy card (above 250 gsm) can crack along the fold.
Start with a sheet folded in half along its long axis to form the
. A5 card folded to A6 is a practical working size.
Cutting and folding the basic tab
With the card closed, mark two parallel lines from the spine fold toward the open edges: 20 mm long, 20 mm apart. These are the cut lines.
Cut along both marked lines with the craft knife and ruler. Cut through both layers simultaneously while the card is closed.
Open the card. Push the cut tab toward you (away from the spine) and crease the fold at the base of the tab so it stays forward.
Close and re-open the card. The tab should spring forward as the card opens and lie flat when the card is closed.

Cut the top edge of the tab into a shape before folding: a stepped rectangle becomes a staircase, a curved cut becomes a hill or arch. Multiple tabs at different positions along the spine create a layered scene. Each additional tab must have its base fold parallel to the spine fold, or the mechanism will not work.
